contract: Curated questions for /demo/{scenarioId}/reasoning. The frontend fixture mirrors this metadata and adds traced answer fixtures until the backend /reason/curated endpoint is wired.

questions.text: At what Glasl stage does this conflict pivot from objectifiable to intractable, and on which event?
questions.stake: Mediators need to know whether they are still facing loss-of-face dynamics or public threat dynamics.

questions.text: Who is the structural spoiler? Quantify by betweenness centrality on de-escalation paths.
questions.stake: Spoilers are the first actor class a peace process has to identify.
questions.academic_anchor: Stedman (1997), Spoiler Problems in Peace Processes.

questions.text: Why does Friar Lawrence's Track-II mediation fail? Diagnose with French/Raven.
questions.stake: Track-II mediators often have expertise and trust but no enforceable authority.
questions.academic_anchor: French & Raven (1959), The Bases of Social Power.

questions.text: If Mercutio survives Act III Scene 1, project the conflict trajectory.
questions.stake: Counterfactual discipline is the daily work of intelligence and mediation analysis.

questions.text: Is the conflict ripe for resolution at the moment of the secret marriage? Compute MHS and MEO.
questions.stake: Mistimed mediation burns credibility before the conflict reaches a hurting stalemate.

questions.text: Which real-world conflicts share this actor-event-norm topology?
questions.stake: Structured comparison turns analyst memory into computable pattern recognition.
questions.academic_anchor: George & Bennett (2005), Case Studies and Theory Development.

questions.text: What unstated interest does Lord Capulet hold that the play never makes explicit?
questions.stake: Fisher/Ury negotiation depends on detecting interests behind stated positions.

questions.text: What intervention would Lederach's conflict transformation framework recommend, and at what timing?
questions.stake: Transformation requires sequencing personal, relational, structural, and cultural work.
questions.academic_anchor: Lederach (2003), The Little Book of Conflict Transformation.

questions.text: How much variance in the 1812 outcome is explained by individual agency vs structural forces?
questions.stake: This operationalizes Tolstoy's great-man critique as a testable causal decomposition.

questions.text: Was Kutuzov's decision to abandon Moscow rational? Compute his BATNA.
questions.stake: Military withdrawal can be a dominant strategy when attrition curves matter more than territory.
questions.academic_anchor: Fisher & Ury (1981); Brodie (1973), War and Politics.

questions.text: Glasl trajectory of the Napoleon-Russia conflict: when does it cross stage 7 -> 8 -> 9?
questions.stake: A literary arc becomes dated stage boundaries in an escalation model.

questions.text: Does the partisan irregular fighter materially shift the outcome, or is Tolstoy's portrayal decorative?
questions.stake: Irregular warfare can be a causal layer, not just a colourful narrative detail.

questions.text: Counterfactual: if Napoleon retreats from Smolensk in August 1812, does France retain Continental hegemony through 1815?
questions.stake: Long horizon counterfactuals must show uncertainty rather than a confident alternate history.
questions.academic_anchor: Pearl (2009); Schroeder (1994), The Transformation of European Politics.

questions.text: Map structural similarity to other imperial overreach cases.
questions.stake: Buyers need cross-case retrieval by operational class, not keyword resemblance.
questions.academic_anchor: Kennedy (1987), The Rise and Fall of the Great Powers.

questions.text: What does Mayer trust analysis say about the Napoleon-Alexander I dyad before, during, and after?
questions.stake: Trust collapse can be decomposed into ability, benevolence, and integrity signals.

questions.text: Did Russia's September 2015 intervention cause regime survival? Quantify the counterfactual.
questions.stake: This is a live policy debate about intervention efficacy and regime survival.
questions.academic_anchor: Charap & Geist (2018), Russia's Use of Military Power; Pearl (2009).

questions.text: When did the conflict cross from Glasl 7 to Glasl 8? Anchor the date on a norm-violation event.
questions.stake: Escalation-stage labels should be tied to events, not adjectives.

questions.text: Why did Russia, Iran, and Turkey bypass Geneva and create the Astana process in 2017? Diagnose the BATNA shift.
questions.stake: Forum choice is often BATNA arithmetic disguised as diplomatic creativity.
questions.academic_anchor: Hill (2018) on Astana; Fisher & Ury BATNA framework.

questions.text: Spoiler typology: which armed groups acted as systematic de-escalation spoilers from 2012-2024?
questions.stake: Different spoiler classes require coercion, isolation, or incentives.

questions.text: Galtung structural-violence index: change from pre-2011 to 2024. Decompose by direct, structural, cultural.
questions.stake: Negative peace is not positive peace; reconstruction planning depends on the difference.
questions.academic_anchor: Galtung (1969), Violence, Peace, and Peace Research.

questions.text: Power asymmetries between Iran-Russia-Turkey-US-Israel as of 2024: French/Raven decomposition.
questions.stake: Leverage is multidimensional; coercive power and legitimacy can diverge sharply.

questions.text: The November-December 2024 HTS offensive: classify the regime collapse as Glasl 9 or Kriesberg settlement.
questions.stake: Collapse, settlement, and mutual destruction lead to very different policy postures.

questions.text: Recurrence probability within 5 years: compute Walter base rate plus structural similarity adjustment.
questions.stake: A recurrence estimate changes donor posture from rebuilding to prevention.
